## 1. Executive Summary

> A concise 2-3 paragraph summary for executive and non-technical stakeholders.

[Organization / Platform Name]'s data architecture provides a unified, governed, and scalable foundation for collecting, storing, transforming, and delivering data across the enterprise. This document describes the design, components, and standards that govern how data flows from its origins through to consumption by analysts, applications, and machine learning systems.

**Key outcomes this architecture enables:**

- [Outcome 1 — e.g., single source of truth for all customer data across 12 business units]
- [Outcome 2 — e.g., sub-5-second latency for operational dashboards]
- [Outcome 3 — e.g., GDPR and CCPA compliance with full data lineage]
- [Outcome 4 — e.g., self-service analytics for 200+ business users]
- [Outcome 5 — e.g., ML feature store enabling model deployment in < 1 day]

---

## 2. Introduction

### 2.1 Purpose

This document defines the data architecture for [System / Platform Name]. It serves as the authoritative reference for data engineers, analysts, architects, and governance teams when designing, building, or evaluating data systems.

### 2.2 Scope

| In Scope | Out of Scope |
|---|---|
| Data ingestion from [source systems] | Application-level database schemas (see App Architecture doc) |
| Data warehouse and data lake design | Front-end BI tool configuration details |
| ETL/ELT pipeline design and standards | Individual report or dashboard specs |
| Data governance, quality, and lineage | ML model training specifics (see ML Platform doc) |
| Real-time streaming architecture | Network-level infrastructure (see Infra doc) |

### 2.3 Intended Audience

| Audience | Primary Sections |
|---|---|
| Chief Data Officer / VP Data | 1, 3, 12, 22, 25 |
| Data Architects | All |
| Data Engineers | 6, 7, 8, 9, 17, 18 |
| Analytics Engineers | 8, 10, 11 |
| Data Analysts / Scientists | 10, 11, 15 |
| Security & Compliance | 14, 21 |
| Platform / DevOps Engineers | 18, 19, 20 |

### 2.4 Relationship to Other Documents

| Document | Relationship |
|---|---|
| [Reference Architecture Doc] | Parent; this doc elaborates on the Data layer |
| [Application Architecture Doc] | Source systems and transactional schemas |
| [ML Platform Doc] | Consumes feature store and data products defined here |
| [Security Architecture Doc] | Security controls applied to data systems |
| [Data Dictionary / Catalog] | Per-entity field definitions; linked throughout |

---

## 3. Business Context & Data Strategy

### 3.1 Business Drivers

- [Driver 1 — e.g., executive decision-making requires reliable, consolidated reporting]
- [Driver 2 — e.g., regulatory requirements mandate data lineage and audit trails]
- [Driver 3 — e.g., data products are a revenue stream via external APIs]
- [Driver 4 — e.g., ML use cases require curated, high-quality feature datasets]

### 3.2 Strategic Data Objectives

| Objective | Description | Success Metric | Target Date |
|---|---|---|---|
| [Obj 1 — e.g., Unified Customer View] | Single 360° profile per customer | < 1% duplicate customers | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 2 — e.g., Self-Service Analytics] | Business users query data without engineer help | [X]% of reports self-served | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 3 — e.g., Real-Time Insights] | Operational dashboards on live data | < [X]s dashboard latency | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 4 — e.g., Data Monetization] | External data products / APIs | [X] paying API consumers | [Q/YYYY] |

### 3.3 Stakeholders

| Role | Name / Team | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Executive Sponsor | [Name] | Funding, strategic alignment |
| Chief Data Officer | [Name] | Data strategy ownership |
| Data Architecture Owner | [Name] | Design decisions, ADRs |
| Data Engineering Lead | [Name] | Pipeline implementation |
| Data Governance Lead | [Name] | Policies, stewardship |
| Analytics Lead | [Name] | Consumption layer needs |
| Security / Privacy Lead | [Name] | Data security and compliance |
| Domain Data Owners | [Team per domain] | Accountability for domain data |

### 3.4 Constraints

- **Budget:** [e.g., cloud data platform spend cap of $X/month]
- **Technology:** [e.g., must integrate with existing Salesforce and SAP systems]
- **Regulatory:** [e.g., data must not leave the EU; GDPR, HIPAA, PCI-DSS applies]
- **Organizational:** [e.g., data team of X engineers; self-service target for analysts]
- **Timeline:** [e.g., warehouse migration complete by Q3 YYYY]

---

## 4. Data Architecture Principles

| # | Principle | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| D1 | **Data as a Product** | Treat datasets as first-class products with owners, SLAs, documentation, and consumers. |
| D2 | **Single Source of Truth** | Each business concept has one authoritative data source; avoid redundant systems of record. |
| D3 | **Schema on Write for Core, Schema on Read for Exploration** | Enforce structure in the warehouse; allow flexibility in the lake. |
| D4 | **Immutable Raw Data** | Raw ingested data is never modified; all transformations produce new datasets. |
| D5 | **Declarative Transformations** | Use dbt / SQL-based transformations; avoid imperative scripts for business logic. |
| D6 | **Privacy by Design** | PII handled at the point of ingestion; default to minimum necessary data. |
| D7 | **Lineage First** | Every dataset must have traceable lineage from source to consumption. |
| D8 | **Fail Fast on Quality** | Data quality checks run in pipelines; bad data is quarantined, not silently propagated. |
| D9 | **Loosely Coupled Pipelines** | Pipelines depend on contracts (schemas), not on other pipeline internals. |
| D10 | **Automate Everything** | Pipeline deployment, testing, and monitoring are fully automated. |

---

## 5. Data Landscape Overview

### 5.1 High-Level Data Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Sources["Data Sources"]
        OLTP[Operational DBs\nPostgreSQL / MySQL / MongoDB]
        SaaS[SaaS Applications\nSalesforce / HubSpot / Zendesk]
        EVENTS[Event Streams\nKafka / Kinesis]
        FILES[Files & Feeds\nCSV / JSON / XML / SFTP]
        EXT[External Data\nAPIs / Partners / Open Data]
    end

    subgraph Ingestion["Ingestion Layer"]
        CDC[CDC\ne.g. Debezium / Fivetran]
        BATCH[Batch ETL/ELT\ne.g. Airbyte / Stitch]
        STREAM[Stream Ingestion\ne.g. Kafka Connect]
    end

    subgraph Storage["Storage Layer"]
        RAW[Raw / Bronze Layer\nUnmodified source data]
        STAGED[Staged / Silver Layer\nCleaned & conformed]
        CURATED[Curated / Gold Layer\nBusiness-ready datasets]
        LAKE[Data Lake\ne.g. S3 / GCS / ADLS]
        DWH[Data Warehouse\ne.g. Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift]
        STORE[Feature Store\ne.g. Feast / Tecton]
    end

    subgraph Transform["Transformation Layer"]
        DBT[dbt / Spark / Flink\nSQL & code transforms]
    end

    subgraph Serving["Serving Layer"]
        BI[BI & Dashboards\nLooker / Metabase / Tableau]
        API[Data APIs\nREST / GraphQL]
        ML[ML Training &\nInference]
        SELF[Self-Service\nSQL / Notebooks]
    end

    Sources --> Ingestion
    Ingestion --> RAW
    RAW --> LAKE
    LAKE --> DBT
    DBT --> STAGED
    STAGED --> CURATED
    CURATED --> DWH
    CURATED --> STORE
    DWH --> Serving
    STORE --> ML
```

### 5.2 Architecture Pattern

> Select the pattern(s) that apply and elaborate.

| Pattern | Adopted? | Notes |
|---|---|---|
| **Lambda Architecture** (batch + speed layers) | [Yes / No] | [Rationale] |
| **Kappa Architecture** (stream-only) | [Yes / No] | [Rationale] |
| **Data Lakehouse** (lake + warehouse semantics) | [Yes / No] | [e.g., using Delta Lake / Iceberg / Hudi] |
| **Data Mesh** (domain-oriented, federated) | [Yes / No] | [Number of domains, federated governance model] |
| **Data Fabric** (integrated metadata layer) | [Yes / No] | [Rationale] |

### 5.3 Technology Stack Summary

| Layer | Technology | Version / Tier | Purpose |
|---|---|---|---|
| Ingestion (batch) | [Fivetran / Airbyte / Glue] | [version] | SaaS & DB replication |
| Ingestion (stream) | [Kafka / Kinesis / Pub/Sub] | [version] | Event streaming |
| CDC | [Debezium / DMS] | [version] | DB change capture |
| Data Lake | [S3 / GCS / ADLS] | [tier config] | Raw & processed storage |
| Table Format | [Delta Lake / Apache Iceberg / Hudi] | [version] | ACID on lake |
| Data Warehouse | [Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift] | [edition] | Analytical queries |
| Transformation | [dbt / Spark / Flink] | [version] | ELT & stream processing |
| Orchestration | [Airflow / Prefect / Dagster] | [version] | Pipeline scheduling |
| BI / Visualization | [Looker / Metabase / Tableau] | [version] | Dashboards, reports |
| Data Catalog | [DataHub / Alation / Glue Catalog] | [version] | Metadata & lineage |
| Data Quality | [Great Expectations / Soda / dbt tests] | [version] | Quality checks |
| Feature Store | [Feast / Tecton / Vertex AI] | [version] | ML features |
| Secrets / Config | [Vault / AWS Secrets Manager] | [version] | Credentials |

---

## 6. Data Sources & Ingestion

### 6.1 Source Inventory

| Source ID | Source Name | Type | System | Data Domain | Volume | Frequency | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| SRC-001 | [e.g., CRM — Salesforce] | SaaS | Salesforce | Customers, Leads | ~[X]K rows/day | Every 1h | [Sales Ops] |
| SRC-002 | [e.g., Orders DB] | OLTP | PostgreSQL | Orders, Line Items | ~[X]M rows/day | CDC (real-time) | [Backend Eng] |
| SRC-003 | [e.g., App Events] | Stream | Kafka | User Behaviour | ~[X]M events/day | Real-time | [Product Eng] |
| SRC-004 | [e.g., Finance — ERP] | On-prem | SAP | GL, Cost Centres | ~[X]K rows/day | Daily batch | [Finance] |
| SRC-005 | [e.g., Partner Feed] | File | SFTP / S3 | [Domain] | [X] files/day | Daily | [Partnerships] |
| SRC-006 | [Source Name] | [Type] | [System] | [Domain] | [Volume] | [Frequency] | [Owner] |

### 6.2 Ingestion Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph BatchIngestion["Batch Ingestion"]
        B1[Full Load\nInitial / periodic full extract]
        B2[Incremental\nDelta based on updated_at or watermark]
        B3[File Drop\nSFTP / S3 landing zone]
    end

    subgraph StreamIngestion["Stream Ingestion"]
        S1[CDC\nDebezium captures DB WAL]
        S2[Event Stream\nApp publishes to Kafka topic]
        S3[Webhook\nThird party pushes events]
    end

    subgraph Landing["Landing Zone\nRaw / Bronze"]
        L1[S3 / GCS\nParquet / JSON / Avro]
    end

    BatchIngestion --> Landing
    StreamIngestion --> Landing
```

### 6.3 Ingestion Standards

- **File formats:** Parquet (default), Avro (streaming), JSON (raw webhooks), CSV (legacy only)
- **Compression:** Snappy for Parquet; gzip for JSON/CSV
- **Partitioning:** By `ingestion_date` at minimum; add business partition (e.g., `region`, `source_id`) where cardinality is appropriate
- **Schema registry:** [Confluent Schema Registry / AWS Glue Schema Registry] — all Avro/Protobuf schemas registered
- **Idempotency:** All ingestion jobs must be re-runnable without duplicating data
- **Late-arriving data:** [X]-hour window for late data; beyond that requires backfill process
- **Error handling:** Failed records routed to dead-letter queue / error bucket; alerting on threshold breach

### 6.4 CDC Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    DB[(Source DB\nPostgreSQL / MySQL)]
    DEB[Debezium\nConnector]
    KAFKA[Kafka Topic\ndb.schema.table]
    CONS[Kafka Consumer\nSpark Streaming / Flink]
    RAW[Raw Layer\nS3 / GCS]
    DWH[(Data Warehouse)]

    DB -- WAL / binlog --> DEB
    DEB --> KAFKA
    KAFKA --> CONS
    CONS --> RAW
    RAW --> DWH
```

---

## 7. Data Storage Architecture

### 7.1 Storage Tiers (Medallion Architecture)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Systems] --> BRZ

    subgraph BRZ["Bronze (Raw)"]
        B1[Exact copy of source\nImmutable\nNo transformations\nFull history retained]
    end

    subgraph SLV["Silver (Staged)"]
        S1[Cleaned & deduplicated\nConformed types & names\nPII masked\nJoined where appropriate]
    end

    subgraph GLD["Gold (Curated)"]
        G1[Business-ready\nAggregated / denormalized\nOwned by data products\nSLA-backed]
    end

    BRZ --> SLV
    SLV --> GLD

    GLD --> BI[BI & Reporting]
    GLD --> API[Data APIs]
    GLD --> ML[ML / AI]
```

| Tier | Also Called | Format | Location | Retention | Who Writes | Who Reads |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Bronze | Raw, Landing | JSON / Avro / Parquet (original) | S3/GCS `raw/` prefix | [X] years | Ingestion pipelines | Data engineers only |
| Silver | Staged, Cleansed | Parquet / Delta / Iceberg | S3/GCS `staged/` prefix | [X] years | dbt / Spark | Data engineers, advanced analysts |
| Gold | Curated, Mart | Parquet / DWH tables | Data Warehouse | [X] years | dbt / pipeline | All consumers |

### 7.2 Data Store Selection

| Store | Technology | Use Case | Consistency | Scaling Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| OLTP / Source | [PostgreSQL / MySQL / MongoDB] | Transactional app data | Strong (ACID) | Vertical + read replicas |
| Data Lake | [S3 / GCS / ADLS Gen2] | Raw and processed bulk storage | Eventual | Infinite (object store) |
| Table Format | [Delta Lake / Apache Iceberg] | ACID transactions on lake | Snapshot isolation | Partition pruning, Z-ordering |
| Data Warehouse | [Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift] | Analytical SQL queries | Eventual (DML ACID) | Virtual warehouse / slot scaling |
| Operational Analytics | [ClickHouse / Apache Druid] | Low-latency aggregations at high QPS | Eventual | Sharding + replication |
| Streaming Store | [Kafka / Kinesis] | Event log, replay, fan-out | At-least-once / exactly-once | Partition scaling |
| Cache | [Redis / Memcached] | Hot lookup data, session, counters | Eventual | Cluster mode |
| Search | [Elasticsearch / OpenSearch] | Full-text, faceted search | Eventual | Shard scaling |
| Graph | [Neo4j / Amazon Neptune] | Relationship-heavy queries | ACID | Cluster |
| Time Series | [InfluxDB / TimescaleDB] | Metrics, telemetry, IoT | Eventual | Retention + downsampling |
| Feature Store | [Feast / Tecton / Vertex AI] | Online/offline ML features | Eventual (offline), strong (online) | Managed |

### 7.3 Partitioning & Clustering Strategy

| Dataset | Partition Key | Cluster / Sort Key | Rationale |
|---|---|---|---|
| [events table] | `event_date` (daily) | `user_id`, `event_type` | Prune by date; fast user lookups |
| [orders table] | `order_date` (monthly) | `customer_id` | Wide time queries; customer-centric |
| [logs table] | `log_date` (daily), `service` | `severity`, `trace_id` | Filter by service and severity |
| [Custom table] | [partition key] | [cluster key] | [Rationale] |

## 8. Data Processing & Transformation

### 8.1 Transformation Layer Overview

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    RAW[Bronze Layer\nRaw Data]

    subgraph DBT["dbt / Transformation Layer"]
        STG[Staging Models\nRename, cast, light clean]
        INT[Intermediate Models\nJoins, business logic]
        MART[Data Mart Models\nFact & dimension tables]
    end

    subgraph SPARK["Spark / Flink\nHeavy Processing"]
        SPARK1[Large-scale joins\nML feature engineering\nComplex aggregations]
    end

    RAW --> STG
    STG --> INT
    INT --> MART
    RAW --> SPARK1
    SPARK1 --> MART

    MART --> DWH[(Data Warehouse\nGold Layer)]
```

### 8.2 Transformation Standards (dbt)

**Project structure:**
```
dbt_project/
├── models/
│   ├── staging/          # stg_<source>__<entity>.sql  (1:1 with source tables)
│   │   ├── salesforce/
│   │   └── postgres/
│   ├── intermediate/     # int_<entity>_<description>.sql
│   ├── marts/            # mart_<domain>__<name>.sql
│   │   ├── core/
│   │   ├── finance/
│   │   └── marketing/
│   └── metrics/          # MetricFlow definitions
├── macros/               # Reusable Jinja macros
├── tests/                # Custom data tests
├── seeds/                # Static reference data
├── snapshots/            # SCD Type 2 snapshots
└── analyses/             # Ad-hoc exploratory SQL
```

**Naming conventions:**

| Layer | Prefix | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Source | `src_` | `src_salesforce` |
| Staging | `stg_` | `stg_salesforce__accounts` |
| Intermediate | `int_` | `int_customers_with_orders` |
| Mart / Fact | `fct_` | `fct_orders` |
| Mart / Dimension | `dim_` | `dim_customers` |
| Mart / Report | `rpt_` | `rpt_monthly_revenue` |

### 8.3 Spark / Heavy Processing Jobs

| Job | Framework | Trigger | Input | Output | SLA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| [Daily User Aggregation] | [PySpark / Scala] | Daily @ [03:00 UTC] | Silver events | Gold `user_daily_stats` | < [X]h |
| [ML Feature Engineering] | [PySpark] | Daily @ [04:00 UTC] | Silver orders, users | Feature Store | < [X]h |
| [Large-Scale Dedup] | [Spark] | Weekly | Bronze CRM dump | Silver customers | < [X]h |
| [Job Name] | [Framework] | [Trigger] | [Input] | [Output] | [SLA] |

### 8.4 Slowly Changing Dimensions (SCD)

| Entity | SCD Type | Strategy | Tool |
|---|---|---|---|
| [Customer] | Type 2 | Full history, `valid_from` / `valid_to` | dbt snapshots |
| [Product] | Type 1 | Overwrite; no history needed | dbt model |
| [Price List] | Type 2 | Full history | dbt snapshots |
| [Entity] | [Type 1/2/3] | [Strategy] | [Tool] |

## 9. Data Flow & Pipelines

### 9.1 End-to-End Pipeline — Operational Data

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as Source DB
    participant DEB as Debezium (CDC)
    participant KFK as Kafka Topic
    participant ING as Ingestion Job
    participant BRZ as Bronze (S3)
    participant DBT as dbt Transform
    participant DWH as Data Warehouse
    participant BI as BI Tool

    SRC->>DEB: Row insert / update / delete
    DEB->>KFK: Publish change event (Avro)
    KFK->>ING: Consume event (micro-batch or streaming)
    ING->>BRZ: Write Parquet to raw zone
    Note over BRZ: Partitioned by ingestion_date
    BRZ->>DBT: dbt incremental model runs (scheduled)
    DBT->>DWH: Upsert into Silver then Gold tables
    DWH->>BI: Dashboard queries Gold layer
```

### 9.2 End-to-End Pipeline — Batch Analytics

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant SRC as SaaS / ERP
    participant AIR as Airflow DAG
    participant ING as Fivetran / Airbyte
    participant BRZ as Bronze (DWH raw schema)
    participant DBT as dbt Models
    participant GLD as Gold Layer
    participant RPT as BI / Report

    AIR->>ING: Trigger connector sync
    ING->>BRZ: Load incremental rows
    AIR->>DBT: Trigger dbt run (staging → mart)
    DBT->>GLD: Build / refresh mart tables
    AIR->>AIR: Run data quality checks
    GLD->>RPT: Analysts / dashboards query
```

### 9.3 Pipeline Inventory

| Pipeline ID | Pipeline Name | Type | Source | Destination | Schedule | Owner | SLA |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| PL-001 | [Orders CDC to DWH] | Stream / CDC | PostgreSQL `orders` | DWH `fct_orders` | Continuous | [Team] | < 5 min lag |
| PL-002 | [Salesforce CRM Sync] | Batch (Fivetran) | Salesforce | DWH raw schema | Hourly | [Team] | < 2h |
| PL-003 | [Daily Revenue Mart] | Batch (dbt) | Silver tables | `mart_finance__revenue` | Daily 06:00 UTC | [Team] | < 07:00 UTC |
| PL-004 | [ML Feature Pipeline] | Batch (Spark) | Silver events | Feature Store | Daily 04:00 UTC | [Team] | < 06:00 UTC |
| PL-005 | [Pipeline Name] | [Type] | [Source] | [Destination] | [Schedule] | [Team] | [SLA] |

### 9.4 Orchestration

- **Tool:** [Apache Airflow / Prefect / Dagster / dbt Cloud]
- **DAG versioning:** DAGs stored in Git; deployed via CI/CD
- **Retry policy:** Max [X] retries with [X]-minute exponential backoff
- **SLA monitoring:** Airflow SLA Miss alerts to [Slack channel / PagerDuty]
- **Backfill strategy:** Parameterized by `start_date` / `end_date`; backfills run in isolation
- **Environment promotion:** DAGs promoted dev → staging → prod via pull request

## 10. Data Modeling

### 10.1 Modeling Approaches by Layer

| Layer | Modeling Approach | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Bronze | Source-aligned, raw | Preserve original structure; no assumptions |
| Silver | 3NF / normalized | Eliminate redundancy; support varied consumption |
| Gold | Dimensional (Kimball) | Optimized for BI query patterns |
| Operational Analytics | Wide / denormalized | Minimize joins; maximize query speed |
| ML Features | Feature vectors | Aligned to model input requirements |

### 10.4 Naming Conventions

| Object | Convention | Example |
|---|---|---|
| Schema / Dataset | lowercase, underscores | `raw_salesforce`, `mart_finance` |
| Table | lowercase, underscores, plural | `orders`, `dim_customers` |
| Column | lowercase, underscores | `customer_id`, `created_at` |
| Primary Key | `<entity>_id` or `<entity>_key` (surrogate) | `customer_id`, `customer_key` |
| Foreign Key | Matching PK name of referenced table | `customer_id` → `customers.customer_id` |
| Timestamps | `_at` suffix (UTC) | `created_at`, `updated_at`, `deleted_at` |
| Boolean | `is_` or `has_` prefix | `is_active`, `has_subscription` |
| Date only | `_date` suffix | `order_date`, `birth_date` |

## 13. Data Quality

### 13.1 Data Quality Dimensions

| Dimension | Definition | Measurement | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Completeness** | Required fields are populated | % non-null on key columns | > [X]% |
| **Accuracy** | Values match source of truth | Cross-source reconciliation | [X]% match |
| **Consistency** | No contradictions across datasets | Cross-dataset assertion tests | 0 failures |
| **Timeliness** | Data is available within SLA | Pipeline lag monitoring | < [X] min / hours |
| **Uniqueness** | No unintended duplicates | Duplicate row count | 0 duplicates on PK |
| **Validity** | Values conform to format / domain | Regex / range tests | [X]% valid |
| **Referential Integrity** | FK relationships are valid | Orphan row count | 0 orphans |

### 13.2 Data Quality Framework

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    INGEST[Ingested Data] --> SCHEMA[Schema Validation\nColumn types, required fields]
    SCHEMA --> RULE[Business Rule Tests\nRange checks, regex, enums]
    RULE --> STAT[Statistical Tests\nAnomaly detection, distribution shift]
    STAT --> CROSS[Cross-Dataset Tests\nRow counts, reconciliation]

    SCHEMA -- fail --> QUAR[Quarantine\nFailed records isolated]
    RULE -- fail --> QUAR
    STAT -- fail --> ALERT[Alert\nSlack / PagerDuty]
    CROSS -- fail --> ALERT

    QUAR --> REVIEW[Human Review\nFix & reprocess or reject]
    ALERT --> REVIEW
```

### 13.3 Quality Checks by Layer

| Layer | Check Type | Tool | Failure Action |
|---|---|---|---|
| Bronze (on ingest) | Schema conformance, not-null PKs | [Great Expectations / dbt tests] | Quarantine + alert |
| Silver (post-clean) | Uniqueness, referential integrity, range | [dbt tests / Soda] | Block promotion + alert |
| Gold (post-transform) | Row count vs prior run, metric reconciliation | [dbt tests / custom] | Pause dashboard + alert |
| Serving layer | SLA checks, freshness | [dbt source freshness] | Alert consumers |

### 13.4 Incident & Remediation Process

1. **Detection** — automated quality check fails; alert fires to [Slack / PagerDuty]
2. **Triage** — on-call data engineer reviews quarantine bucket and alert context
3. **Classification** — severity assigned: P1 (Gold broken), P2 (Silver broken), P3 (Bronze only)
4. **Containment** — affected downstream datasets flagged; dashboards show "data delayed" banner
5. **Root Cause** — investigate source, ingestion job, or transform logic
6. **Fix & Reprocess** — fix deployed; backfill run for affected partition(s)
7. **Post-mortem** — documented for P1/P2 incidents; learnings fed back into quality rules

## 14. Data Security & Privacy

### 14.1 Data Classification

| Classification | Description | Examples | Controls |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Public** | Safe for external sharing | Aggregated reports, open data | None beyond standard |
| **Internal** | Internal use only | Business metrics, non-PII tables | Auth required |
| **Confidential** | Sensitive business data | Revenue, pricing, HR data | RBAC + audit log |
| **Restricted / PII** | Personal or regulated data | Email, name, SSN, health data | Encryption + masking + audit |

### 14.2 PII Handling

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Source Data\nwith PII]
    
    subgraph Ingestion["At Ingestion"]
        DETECT[PII Detection\ne.g. regex, NLP scan]
        MASK[Masking / Tokenization\nreplace PII with token]
        ENCRYPT[Encryption\nAES-256 at rest]
    end

    subgraph Storage["In Storage"]
        RAW_PII[Raw Bronze\nEncrypted, restricted access]
        SILVER_MASKED[Silver & Gold\nMasked / tokenized values]
    end

    subgraph Serving["At Serving"]
        DYNMASK[Dynamic Masking\nRole-based column masking]
    end

    SRC --> DETECT --> MASK --> ENCRYPT
    ENCRYPT --> RAW_PII
    MASK --> SILVER_MASKED
    SILVER_MASKED --> DYNMASK
```

### 14.3 PII Field Inventory (Sample)

| Field | Table(s) | Classification | Treatment | Retention |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| `email` | `customers`, `stg_crm__contacts` | PII | SHA-256 hash in Silver+ | Delete on request |
| `full_name` | `customers` | PII | Tokenized | Delete on request |
| `phone_number` | `customers`, `orders` | PII | Masked (`***-***-1234`) | Delete on request |
| `ip_address` | `events` | PII / Quasi-identifier | Truncated to /24 | [X] days |
| `date_of_birth` | `customers` | PII | Year only in Silver+ | Delete on request |
| `payment_card_*` | — | PCI — Not stored | Tokenized via Stripe | Never stored |
| `[field]` | `[table]` | [Class] | [Treatment] | [Retention] |

### 14.4 Encryption Standards

| Context | Standard | Managed By |
|---|---|---|
| Data at rest (object store) | AES-256 (SSE-S3 / CMEK) | [Cloud KMS] |
| Data at rest (DWH) | AES-256 (platform-managed or CMEK) | [Snowflake / BigQuery] |
| Data in transit | TLS 1.2+ | Platform default |
| Column-level encryption | [e.g., Snowflake column encryption / BigQuery AEAD] | [Data Eng] |
| Key rotation | Every [X] days | [Vault / KMS automated] |

### 14.5 Audit & Access Logging

- All data access to Restricted / Confidential data is logged
- Log fields: `user_id`, `role`, `action` (SELECT/INSERT/DELETE), `table`, `row_count`, `timestamp`, `query_hash`
- Log retention: [X] years
- Log store: [CloudTrail / Snowflake Access History / BigQuery Audit Logs]
- Anomaly alerting: Unusual access patterns trigger alert within [X] minutes

## 17. Real-Time vs Batch Architecture

### 17.1 Decision Framework

| Criteria | Batch | Micro-Batch | Real-Time Streaming |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data latency requirement | Hours to days | Minutes | Seconds or less |
| Complexity | Low | Medium | High |
| Cost | Low | Medium | Higher |
| Use case examples | Monthly reports, ML training | Hourly dashboards, CDC sync | Fraud detection, live alerts |
| Technology | dbt + Airflow | Spark Structured Streaming | Flink / Kafka Streams |

### 17.2 Lambda Architecture (if applicable)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    SRC[Event Stream\nKafka]

    subgraph Batch["Batch Layer (Accuracy)"]
        BL[Spark Batch Job\nFull historical recompute\nRuns nightly]
        BV[Batch Views\nHigh-accuracy, higher latency]
    end

    subgraph Speed["Speed Layer (Recency)"]
        SL[Flink / Spark Streaming\nIncremental real-time processing]
        SV[Real-Time Views\nLower accuracy, low latency]
    end

    subgraph Serving["Serving Layer"]
        MRG[Merge / Union\nBatch view + delta from speed]
        QRY[Query Interface]
    end

    SRC --> Batch
    SRC --> Speed
    Batch --> MRG
    Speed --> MRG
    MRG --> QRY
```

### 17.3 Streaming Pipeline Design

| Component | Technology | Config |
|---|---|---|
| Message broker | [Kafka / Kinesis] | [X] partitions, replication factor [X] |
| Schema registry | [Confluent / Glue] | Avro schemas, compatibility: BACKWARD |
| Stream processor | [Flink / Spark Streaming / Kafka Streams] | Checkpointing every [X]s, exactly-once semantics |
| State store | [RocksDB (Flink) / Redis] | [TTL / size config] |
| Sink | [S3 / DWH / Redis] | Micro-batch commit every [X]s |
| Dead letter queue | [Kafka DLQ topic / SQS DLQ] | Alert on message count > [X] |

```mermaid
                S3[Object Store\nS3 / GCS / ADLS]
                DWH[Data Warehouse\nSnowflake / BigQuery / Redshift]
                KFK[Kafka\nMSK / Confluent Cloud]
            end

            subgraph Services["Supporting Services"]
                KMS[KMS / Secrets]
                IAM[IAM / Service Accounts]
                MON[Monitoring\nCloudWatch / Stackdriver]
            end
        end
    end
```

### 18.2 Infrastructure as Code

- **Tool:** [Terraform / Pulumi / CDK]
- **Repository:** [Link to infra repo]
- **State backend:** [S3 + DynamoDB / GCS + lock]
- **Secrets:** Managed via [Vault / AWS Secrets Manager]; never in code
- **Environments:** `dev`, `staging`, `prod` — each in isolated cloud account / project

### 18.3 CI/CD for Data Pipelines

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    PR[Pull Request] --> CI

    subgraph CI["CI Checks"]
        LINT[SQL Lint\nsqlfluff]
        TEST[dbt compile\n+ unit tests]
        SCAN[Security scan\ncredential detection]
        SLIM[dbt slim CI\ntest only changed models]
    end

    CI --> STAGING[Deploy to Staging\ndbt run --target staging]
    STAGING --> INTTEST[Integration Tests\nRow count, freshness, reconciliation]
    INTTEST --> APPROVE{Approval}
    APPROVE --> PROD[Deploy to Production\ndbt run --target prod]
    PROD --> MONITOR[Post-deploy Monitoring\nAlert on anomalies]
```

---

## 19. Observability & Operations

### 19.1 Observability Stack

| Concern | Tool | Alert Channel |
|---|---|---|
| Pipeline failures | [Airflow / Prefect alerting] | [Slack #data-alerts / PagerDuty] |
| Data freshness | [dbt source freshness / Monte Carlo] | [Slack #data-quality] |
| Data quality failures | [Great Expectations / Soda / dbt tests] | [Slack #data-quality / PagerDuty P1] |
| Infrastructure metrics | [CloudWatch / Datadog / Grafana] | [Slack / PagerDuty] |
| Cost anomalies | [AWS Cost Anomaly / Snowflake credit alerts] | [Email + Slack #data-costs] |
| Query performance | [DWH Query History / Execution plans] | [Weekly digest] |

### 19.2 Key Metrics to Monitor

| Metric | Description | Target | Alert Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pipeline success rate | % DAG runs succeeding | > 99% | < 95% over 24h |
| Data freshness lag | Time since last successful load | < [X] min/h | > [X] min/h per SLA |
| Quality test pass rate | % dbt / GE tests passing | 100% on Gold | Any Gold test failure |
| DWH query p95 latency | p95 query execution time | < [X]s | > [X]s for [X] min |
| Kafka consumer lag | Events waiting to be processed | < [X]K messages | > [X]K for [X] min |
| Daily row count delta | % change from prior day | ± [X]% | > ± [X]% |

### 19.3 Runbook Index

| Scenario | Runbook |
|---|---|
| Pipeline DAG failure | [Link to runbook] |
| Data freshness SLA breach | [Link to runbook] |
| DWH warehouse suspended | [Link to runbook] |
| Kafka consumer lag spike | [Link to runbook] |
| PII data exposure incident | [Link to runbook] |
| Emergency backfill procedure | [Link to runbook] |

---

## 20. Scalability & Performance

### 20.1 Scalability Targets

| Component | Current Load | 1-Year Target | Scaling Mechanism |
|---|---|---|---|
| Daily ingestion volume | [X] GB/day | [X] GB/day | Partitioned object store, auto-scaling |
| DWH concurrent queries | [X] | [X] | Multi-cluster / additional slots |
| Kafka peak throughput | [X] MB/s | [X] MB/s | Add partitions + brokers |
| Spark job max dataset | [X] TB | [X] TB | Autoscaling cluster |
| Airflow parallel tasks | [X] | [X] | Celery / Kubernetes executor |

### 20.2 Query Performance Patterns

| Pattern | Implementation | Use Case |
|---|---|---|
| Partition pruning | Partition tables by date / region | Avoid full scans on large tables |
| Clustering / Z-ordering | Cluster on high-cardinality filter columns | Fast point lookups |
| Materialized aggregates | Pre-computed summary tables in Gold | Dashboard acceleration |
| Result caching | DWH query result cache (Snowflake / BQ) | Repeated identical queries |
| Approximate aggregations | `APPROX_COUNT_DISTINCT`, HyperLogLog | Count distinct at scale |
| Incremental models | dbt incremental strategy | Avoid full rebuilds |

| Right | Trigger | Process | SLA |

## 22. Cost Management

### 22.1 Cost Breakdown (Estimated)

| Category | Service | Est. Monthly Cost | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Data Warehouse | [Snowflake / BigQuery / Redshift] | $[X] | [X] credits / TB scanned |
| Object Storage | [S3 / GCS] | $[X] | [X] TB stored |
| Stream Processing | [Kafka MSK / Confluent] | $[X] | [X] partitions, [X] GB/day |

| **Total** | | **$[X]/month** | |

### 22.2 Cost Optimization Strategies

- **DWH:** Auto-suspend warehouses after [X] min idle; separate warehouses by workload class
- **Compute:** Spot/preemptible instances for Spark batch; reserved for steady Airflow workers
- **Storage:** Lifecycle policies — transition Bronze to infrequent access after [X] days; glacier after [X] months
- **Query optimization:** Eliminate SELECT *; enforce query cost limits per user role
- **Ingestion:** Avoid full loads where incremental is possible; compress and columnarize at landing
- **Monitoring:** [X]-day rolling cost anomaly detection; budget alerts at [X]% of monthly cap

## 24. Risks & Mitigations

| ID | Risk | Likelihood | Impact | Severity | Mitigation | Owner |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| R01 | Data vendor lock-in (e.g., Snowflake-specific SQL) | Medium | High | High | Use open formats (Parquet/Iceberg); abstract via dbt | [Arch] |
| R02 | PII data breach via over-privileged access | Low | Critical | High | RBAC, column masking, quarterly access reviews | [Security] |
| R03 | Pipeline failure causes stale dashboards | High | High | High | SLA monitoring, freshness checks, fallback banners | [Data Eng] |
| R04 | Schema change in source breaks downstream | High | Medium | High | Schema registry, contract testing, alerting | [Data Eng] |
| R05 | Uncontrolled DWH cost growth | Medium | Medium | Medium | Query cost limits, auto-suspend, budget alerts | [Data Arch] |
| R06 | Data quality issues silently propagate | Medium | High | High | Quality gates at each tier; block on failure | [Data Eng] |
| R07 | Key person dependency on data platform | Medium | High | High | Documentation, cross-training, runbooks | [Data Lead] |
| R08 | [Risk] | [L/M/H] | [L/M/H/Crit] | [L/M/H] | [Mitigation] | [Owner] |

## 26. Glossary

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| ADR | Architecture Decision Record |
| Backfill | Reprocessing historical data for a given time range |
| Bronze | Raw, unmodified copy of source data (Medallion tier 1) |
| CDC | Change Data Capture — tracks row-level changes in a source DB |
| CMEK | Customer-Managed Encryption Keys |
| Data Contract | Formal schema + SLA agreement between data producer and consumer |
| Data Lakehouse | Architecture combining data lake storage with data warehouse semantics |
| Data Mesh | Decentralized data architecture where domains own their data products |
| Data Product | A curated, documented, SLA-backed dataset treated as a product |
| dbt | Data Build Tool — SQL-first transformation framework |
| DWH | Data Warehouse |
| ELT | Extract, Load, Transform — load raw first, then transform in-warehouse |
| ETL | Extract, Transform, Load — transform before loading |
| Fact Table | Table of measurable business events in a dimensional model |
| Feature Store | Centralized store for ML features (online + offline) |
| Golden Record | The single authoritative record for a master data entity |
| Iceberg | Apache Iceberg — open table format for large analytic datasets |
| Idempotency | Property where running a job multiple times produces the same result |
| Lineage | Traceable path of data from its origin through all transformations to consumption |
| Medallion | Bronze / Silver / Gold layered architecture pattern |
| MDM | Master Data Management |
| OLAP | Online Analytical Processing — optimized for analytical queries |
| OLTP | Online Transaction Processing — optimized for transactional workloads |
| PII | Personally Identifiable Information |
| RPO | Recovery Point Objective — maximum acceptable data loss |
| RTO | Recovery Time Objective — maximum acceptable downtime |
| SCD | Slowly Changing Dimension — strategy for tracking historical changes |
| SLA | Service Level Agreement |
| Surrogate Key | Artificial key (UUID/integer) used as PK in dimensional models |
| WAL | Write-Ahead Log — transaction log used by relational databases |
| Z-ordering | Data clustering technique used in Delta Lake / Iceberg for fast filtering |
| [Term] | [Definition] |

---

## 1. Executive Summary

> A concise 2-3 paragraph summary for executive and non-technical stakeholders.

[Organization / Platform Name]'s integration architecture defines how all internal systems, external partners, SaaS applications, and consumers exchange data and capabilities in a secure, reliable, and governed manner. This document describes the patterns, standards, platforms, and operational practices that govern all integrations across the enterprise.

**Key outcomes this architecture enables:**

- [Outcome 1 — e.g., a single API gateway as the unified entry point for all external consumers]
- [Outcome 2 — e.g., event-driven decoupling across [X] internal services reducing cascading failures]
- [Outcome 3 — e.g., onboarding a new SaaS integration in < 1 week using standardized patterns]
- [Outcome 4 — e.g., end-to-end integration observability with < 5-minute MTTD on failures]
- [Outcome 5 — e.g., PCI-DSS and GDPR compliant data exchange with all third parties]

---

## 2. Introduction

### 2.1 Purpose

This document defines the integration architecture for [System / Platform Name]. It is the authoritative reference for integration engineers, architects, and platform teams when designing, building, or reviewing any integration between systems — internal or external.

### 2.2 Scope

| In Scope | Out of Scope |
|---|---|
| Internal service-to-service integration | Application-level business logic |
| External API design and publishing | Frontend / UI implementation details |
| Event streaming and messaging architecture | Data warehouse ETL/ELT pipelines (see Data Architecture doc) |
| SaaS, partner, and B2B integrations | Infrastructure networking layer (see Infra doc) |
| API gateway, service mesh, ESB/iPaaS | ML model serving specifics (see ML Platform doc) |
| Integration security, auth, and governance | Individual micro-service internal design |

### 2.3 Intended Audience

| Audience | Primary Sections |
|---|---|
| CTO / Engineering Leadership | 1, 3, 25, 30 |
| Integration / Platform Architects | All |
| Backend / API Engineers | 6, 7, 8, 9, 12, 16 |
| DevOps / Platform Engineers | 12, 13, 22, 23 |
| Security Engineers | 14, 17, 26 |
| Partner / Business Development | 19 |
| QA / Test Engineers | 21 |

### 2.4 Relationship to Other Documents

| Document | Relationship |
|---|---|
| [Reference Architecture Doc] | Parent — this doc elaborates on the Integration layer |
| [Data Architecture Doc] | Data pipelines and ingestion; complements integration patterns |
| [Security Architecture Doc] | Security controls applied at integration boundaries |
| [API Catalog / Developer Portal] | Per-API documentation; linked throughout this doc |
| [Service Inventory] | Inventory of all internal services; consumed by Section 5 |

---

## 3. Business Context & Integration Strategy

### 3.1 Business Drivers

- [Driver 1 — e.g., connecting [X] acquired companies onto the central platform within 12 months]
- [Driver 2 — e.g., enabling a partner ecosystem through published APIs]
- [Driver 3 — e.g., replacing a legacy ESB that is a single point of failure]
- [Driver 4 — e.g., reducing integration development time from weeks to days]
- [Driver 5 — e.g., real-time event sharing across business units to enable cross-sell]

### 3.2 Strategic Integration Objectives

| Objective | Description | Success Metric | Target |
|---|---|---|---|
| [Obj 1 — Unified API layer] | All external-facing capabilities via single gateway | [X]% of APIs through gateway | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 2 — Event-driven decoupling] | Critical services communicate via events, not direct calls | [X] services migrated to async | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 3 — Partner API program] | External developers can self-onboard via developer portal | [X] active external API consumers | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 4 — Integration observability] | Full traceability of every integration call | MTTD < [X] min on failures | [Q/YYYY] |
| [Obj 5] | [Description] | [Metric] | [Date] |

### 3.3 Stakeholders

| Role | Name / Team | Responsibility |
|---|---|---|
| Executive Sponsor | [Name] | Funding, strategic alignment |
| Integration Architecture Owner | [Name] | Design decisions, standards, ADRs |
| Platform / API Engineering Lead | [Name] | Implementation of gateway, mesh, messaging |
| Security Lead | [Name] | Auth standards, API security review |
| Partner Engineering Lead | [Name] | B2B and partner integration delivery |
| Domain Service Owners | [Per-team] | Accountability for their service's API contracts |
| Operations Lead | [Name] | Integration monitoring and incident response |

### 3.4 Constraints

- **Technology:** [e.g., must work with existing SAP ERP and Salesforce CRM]
- **Regulatory:** [e.g., PII must not transit outside EU; PCI data must use tokenization]
- **Organizational:** [e.g., [X] integration engineers; self-service goal for domain teams]
- **Budget:** [e.g., iPaaS/ESB platform cap of $X/month]
- **Timeline:** [e.g., legacy ESB decommission by Q3 YYYY]

---

## 4. Integration Principles

| # | Principle | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| I1 | **API-First** | Define the contract (OpenAPI / AsyncAPI) before writing code. The contract is the product. |
| I2 | **Loose Coupling** | Consumers depend on published contracts, not internal implementations. Services can evolve independently. |
| I3 | **Idempotency** | All integration endpoints must tolerate duplicate calls without side effects. |
| I4 | **Async by Default** | Prefer async messaging for non-blocking workflows; use sync only when a real-time response is required. |
| I5 | **Single Entry Point** | All external traffic enters through the API gateway; no direct service exposure to the internet. |
| I6 | **Fail Gracefully** | Every integration must define fallback behavior: circuit breaker, retry with backoff, or graceful degradation. |
| I7 | **Versioning is Mandatory** | All APIs are versioned from day one; breaking changes require a new version, never a silent modification. |
| I8 | **Observability Built-In** | Every integration call emits a trace ID, structured log, and metric. No dark traffic. |
| I9 | **Security at Every Layer** | Authentication, authorization, and encryption at every integration boundary — internal and external. |
| I10 | **Contract Testing** | Producers and consumers maintain automated contract tests; deployment blocked on contract failure. |
| I11 | **Schema Registry for Events** | All event schemas registered and versioned; consumers fail fast on schema violations. |
| I12 | **Ownership is Clear** | Every integration, API, and topic has a named owner, documented SLA, and on-call contact. |

---

## 5. Integration Landscape Overview

### 5.1 Integration Context Diagram

```mermaid
C4Context

    System_Boundary(platform, "[Platform Name] Integration Layer") {
        System(apigw, "API Gateway", "External-facing: rate limiting, auth, routing")
        System(mesh, "Service Mesh", "Internal: mTLS, observability, traffic control")
        System(broker, "Message Broker", "Async: Kafka / RabbitMQ event bus")
        System(ipaas, "iPaaS / ESB", "SaaS & legacy integration: MuleSoft / Boomi")
    }

    System_Ext(crm, "CRM — Salesforce", "Customer data")
    System_Ext(erp, "ERP — SAP", "Finance, HR, supply chain")
    System_Ext(payment, "Payment Gateway — Stripe", "Payments")
    System_Ext(idp, "Identity Provider — Auth0/Okta", "AuthN / AuthZ")
    System_Ext(notify, "Notifications — SendGrid/Twilio", "Email, SMS, push")
    System_Ext(analytics, "Analytics — Segment / Mixpanel", "Event analytics")
    System_Ext(partnerA, "Partner A", "EDI / SFTP / REST")

    Rel(extdev, apigw, "REST / GraphQL", "HTTPS")
    Rel(partner, ipaas, "EDI / SFTP / REST")
    Rel(internaluser, mesh, "Internal tools")
    Rel(apigw, mesh, "Routes to internal services")
    Rel(mesh, broker, "Service events")
    Rel(ipaas, crm, "Bi-directional sync")
    Rel(ipaas, erp, "Bi-directional sync")
    Rel(mesh, payment, "Payment calls")
    Rel(mesh, idp, "Token validation")
    Rel(mesh, notify, "Send notifications")
    Rel(broker, analytics, "Event stream")
    Rel(ipaas, partnerA, "Partner data exchange")
```

### 5.2 Integration Topology Summary

| Internal service-to-service (sync) | REST / gRPC via service mesh | [Istio / Linkerd] | [X] |

| External API consumers | REST / GraphQL via API gateway | [Kong / AWS APIGW] | [X] |

### 5.3 Technology Stack Summary

| API Gateway (external) | [Kong / AWS API GW / Apigee / Nginx] | [version] | Auth, rate limiting, routing |
| API Gateway (internal) | [Kong / Envoy / Traefik] | [version] | Internal routing, observability |

| Schema Registry | [Confluent Schema Registry / AWS Glue] | [version] | Event schema versioning |
| iPaaS / ESB | [MuleSoft / Boomi / Azure Logic Apps / Zapier] | [version] | SaaS & legacy integration |
| API Design | [Stoplight / Postman / SwaggerHub] | [version] | API-first design & docs |
| Contract Testing | [Pact / Spring Cloud Contract] | [version] | Consumer-driven contracts |
| API Catalog | [Backstage / Developer Portal / Readme.io] | [version] | API discovery & docs |
| Transformation | [JSONata / XSLT / custom mapper] | [version] | Data mapping & conversion |
| MFT (File Transfer) | [GoAnywhere / AWS Transfer Family / SFTP] | [version] | Managed file transfer |

## 6. Integration Patterns & Styles

### 6.1 Pattern Selection Guide

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START([New Integration Need]) --> Q1{Does the caller\nneed an immediate\nresponse?}

    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is it within\nthe same system\nboundary?}
    Q1 -- No --> Q3{Is it data\nsync or event\nnotification?}

    Q2 -- Internal --> GRPC[gRPC\nHigh-performance\ninternal RPC]
    Q2 -- External / Cross-domain --> REST[REST API\nvia API Gateway]

    Q3 -- Event Notification --> EVENT[Event-Driven\nKafka / SNS]
    Q3 -- Data Sync --> Q4{Volume?}

    Q4 -- High volume / bulk --> FILE[File / Batch\nSFTP / S3 drop]
    Q4 -- Low-medium volume --> MSG[Message Queue\nSQS / RabbitMQ]

    REST --> GW[API Gateway]
    GRPC --> MESH[Service Mesh]
    EVENT --> BROKER[Message Broker]
    MSG --> BROKER
    FILE --> MFT[MFT / iPaaS]
```

### 6.2 Integration Pattern Reference

| Pattern | Description | When to Use | When NOT to Use | Technology |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Synchronous REST** | Request → response over HTTP | External APIs, simple CRUD, when caller blocks | High-latency operations, fire-and-forget | HTTP/1.1, HTTP/2 |
| **gRPC / RPC** | Strongly typed binary RPC | High-performance internal calls, streaming | External consumers, browser clients | gRPC + Protobuf |
| **Async Messaging** | Producer publishes; consumer processes independently | Decoupling, workflows, fan-out | When immediate response required | Kafka, SQS, RabbitMQ |
| **Event Streaming** | Continuous ordered log of events | Audit, analytics, replay, event sourcing | Simple point-to-point request/reply | Kafka, Kinesis |
| **Publish / Subscribe** | One-to-many event fan-out | Notifications, cache invalidation, multi-consumer | Ordered processing, exactly-once required | SNS, Kafka, Redis Pub/Sub |
| **Webhooks** | Outbound HTTP push on trigger | Notifying external systems of internal events | High-volume streams, unreliable consumers | HTTPS POST |
| **GraphQL** | Client-specified queries | Flexible client-driven data fetching, BFF pattern | Simple CRUD, internal high-performance calls | Apollo, Hasura |
| **Batch / File** | Bulk transfer of data via file | Legacy integration, high-volume periodic sync | Real-time requirements | SFTP, S3, EDI |
| **CDC** | Capture DB row-level changes as a stream | Data sync without coupling to app API | When source DB access is unavailable | Debezium, DMS |
| **Saga** | Distributed transaction via compensating actions | Long-running multi-service business transactions | Simple single-service operations | Choreography / Orchestration |
| **BFF (Backend for Frontend)** | Dedicated API layer per client type | Web/mobile needing different response shapes | When one API fits all clients | GraphQL / REST aggregator |
| **Strangler Fig** | Incrementally replace legacy system | Migration away from monolith or legacy ESB | Greenfield systems | Proxy / facade |

### 6.3 Synchronous vs Asynchronous Decision Matrix

| Factor | Synchronous | Asynchronous |
|---|---|---|
| Caller needs immediate result | Required | Not possible |
| Operations must be ordered | Best effort | Partition-key ordering |
| Decoupling desired | Low | High |
| Scalability under load | Limited by slowest service | Independent scaling |
| Failure isolation | Cascades to caller | Producer unaffected by consumer failure |
| Complexity | Low | Higher (idempotency, DLQ, ordering) |
| Typical latency | Milliseconds | Milliseconds to seconds |
| Examples | Auth check, payment, search | Order processing, notifications, analytics |

## 7. API Architecture

### 7.1 API Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph External["External Consumers"]
        WEB[Web App]
        MOB[Mobile App]
        THIRD[Third-Party / Partner]
        DEV[Developer / API Consumer]
    end

    subgraph Gateway["API Gateway Layer"]
        GW[API Gateway\nAuth · Rate Limit · Routing · Logging]
        DEV_PORTAL[Developer Portal\nDocs · Keys · Sandbox]
    end

    subgraph BFF["BFF / Aggregation Layer (optional)"]
        BFF_WEB[BFF — Web]
        BFF_MOB[BFF — Mobile]
        GQL[GraphQL Gateway]
    end

    subgraph Services["Internal Services"]
        SVC_A[Service A\nREST / gRPC]
        SVC_B[Service B\nREST / gRPC]
        SVC_C[Service C\nREST / gRPC]
    end

    External --> Gateway
    DEV_PORTAL --> GW
    GW --> BFF
    GW --> Services
    BFF --> Services
```

### 7.2 REST API Design Standards

**URL Structure:**
```
https://api.[domain].com/{version}/{resource}/{id}/{sub-resource}

Examples:
  GET    /v1/customers
  GET    /v1/customers/{id}
  POST   /v1/customers
  PUT    /v1/customers/{id}
  PATCH  /v1/customers/{id}
  DELETE /v1/customers/{id}
  GET    /v1/customers/{id}/orders
```

**HTTP Methods:**

| Method | Use | Idempotent | Safe |
|---|---|---|---|
| GET | Retrieve resource(s) | Yes | Yes |
| POST | Create resource | No | No |
| PUT | Replace resource (full) | Yes | No |
| PATCH | Partial update | No | No |
| DELETE | Remove resource | Yes | No |

**Response Status Codes:**

| Code | Meaning | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| 200 OK | Success | GET, PUT, PATCH |
| 201 Created | Resource created | POST |
| 202 Accepted | Async accepted | Async POST / long-running |
| 204 No Content | Success, no body | DELETE |
| 400 Bad Request | Invalid input | Validation errors |
| 401 Unauthorized | Missing / invalid auth | Unauthenticated call |
| 403 Forbidden | Authenticated but no permission | RBAC rejection |
| 404 Not Found | Resource doesn't exist | GET by ID |
| 409 Conflict | State conflict | Duplicate create, optimistic lock |
| 422 Unprocessable Entity | Semantic validation error | Business rule violation |
| 429 Too Many Requests | Rate limit exceeded | Throttled consumer |
| 500 Internal Server Error | Unexpected server error | Unhandled exception |
| 503 Service Unavailable | Temporary outage | Circuit open, maintenance |

**Standard Response Envelope:**
```json
{
  "data": { },
  "meta": {
    "request_id": "uuid",
    "timestamp": "ISO-8601",
    "version": "1.0"
  },
  "pagination": {
    "cursor": "opaque-cursor",
    "has_more": true,
    "limit": 25
  }
}
```

**Standard Error Response (RFC 7807 Problem Details):**
```json
{
  "type": "https://api.[domain].com/errors/validation-error",
  "title": "Validation Error",
  "status": 400,
  "detail": "The 'email' field must be a valid email address.",
  "instance": "/v1/customers",
  "request_id": "uuid",
  "errors": [
    { "field": "email", "code": "INVALID_FORMAT", "message": "..." }
  ]
}
```

### 7.3 Versioning Strategy

| Strategy | Approach | Example | Status |
|---|---|---|---|
| URI Path (adopted) | Version in URL path | `/v1/customers`, `/v2/customers` | **Adopted** |
| Header versioning | `Accept: application/vnd.api+json;version=2` | — | Not adopted |
| Query param | `?api-version=2` | — | Not adopted |

**Versioning rules:**
- All APIs start at `v1`
- **Non-breaking changes** (additive fields, new optional params) — same version, announced in changelog
- **Breaking changes** (removed fields, changed types, new required params) — new version (`v2`)
- Old versions supported for minimum [X] months after new version GA
- Deprecation communicated via `Deprecation` and `Sunset` response headers

### 7.4 API Design Workflow (API-First)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    REQ[Requirements\n& Use Cases] --> DESIGN[API Design\nOpenAPI / AsyncAPI spec]
    DESIGN --> REVIEW[Design Review\nArchitecture + Security]
    REVIEW --> MOCK[Mock Server\nProtoype & validate with consumers]
    MOCK --> CONTRACT[Contract Tests\nPact / consumer-driven]
    CONTRACT --> IMPL[Implementation\nCode against the spec]
    IMPL --> VALIDATE[Spec Validation\nLint + test in CI]
    VALIDATE --> PUBLISH[Publish to\nAPI Catalog / Developer Portal]
    PUBLISH --> MONITOR[Monitor in Production\nUsage, errors, latency]
```

## 8. Event-Driven Architecture

### 8.1 EDA Overview

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Producers["Event Producers"]
        SVC_A[Order Service]
        SVC_B[Payment Service]
        SVC_C[User Service]
    end

    subgraph Broker["Event Broker (Kafka)"]
        T1[Topic: orders.v1]
        T2[Topic: payments.v1]
        T3[Topic: users.v1]
        DLQ[Dead Letter Topics\n*.dlq]
    end

    subgraph Consumers["Event Consumers"]
        SVC_D[Fulfillment Service]
        SVC_E[Notification Service]
        SVC_F[Analytics Pipeline]
        SVC_G[Audit Service]
    end

    SVC_A -->|publishes| T1
    SVC_B -->|publishes| T2
    SVC_C -->|publishes| T3

    T1 -->|consumes| SVC_D
    T1 -->|consumes| SVC_F
    T2 -->|consumes| SVC_E
    T2 -->|consumes| SVC_F
    T3 -->|consumes| SVC_E
    T3 -->|consumes| SVC_G

    T1 -.->|failed messages| DLQ
    T2 -.->|failed messages| DLQ
```

### 8.2 Event Design Standards

**Event envelope (CloudEvents spec):**
```json
{
  "specversion": "1.0",
  "id": "uuid-v4",
  "source": "/services/order-service",
  "type": "com.[domain].orders.v1.order.placed",
  "subject": "orders/ord_12345",
  "time": "2025-01-15T10:30:00Z",
  "datacontenttype": "application/json",
  "data": {
    "order_id": "ord_12345",
    "customer_id": "cust_67890",
    "total_amount": 99.99,
    "currency": "USD",
    "status": "placed"
  }
}
```

**Event naming convention:**
```
com.[org].[domain].[version].[entity].[verb]

Examples:
  com.acme.orders.v1.order.placed
  com.acme.orders.v1.order.cancelled
  com.acme.payments.v1.payment.completed
  com.acme.users.v1.user.registered
```

**Event design rules:**
- Events describe what **happened** (past tense), not what to do
- Events are **immutable** — once published, never modified; issue a corrective event instead
- Events must be **self-contained** — consumer should not need to call back to get full context
- Events must include **causation_id** (triggering event) and **correlation_id** (business transaction)
- **No PII** in events unless the topic has restricted access and encryption configured

### 8.3 Event Catalog

| Event Type | Producer | Consumers | Schema | Topic | Freq | Retention |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| `orders.v1.order.placed` | Order Service | Fulfillment, Notifications, Analytics | [Link] | `orders.v1` | ~[X]/day | [X] days |
| `payments.v1.payment.completed` | Payment Service | Orders, Notifications, Analytics | [Link] | `payments.v1` | ~[X]/day | [X] days |
| `payments.v1.payment.failed` | Payment Service | Orders, Notifications | [Link] | `payments.v1` | ~[X]/day | [X] days |
| `users.v1.user.registered` | User Service | Notifications, CRM sync, Analytics | [Link] | `users.v1` | ~[X]/day | [X] days |
| `[event.type]` | [Producer] | [Consumers] | [Link] | [Topic] | [~X/day] | [X days] |

### 8.4 Event Sourcing (if applicable)

- **Adopted for:** [List domains — e.g., Orders domain]
- **Event store:** [EventStoreDB / Kafka + compacted topics / custom]
- **Snapshot strategy:** Snapshot every [X] events to limit replay time
- **Projection rebuild:** Projections rebuilt from event log on demand; max rebuild time [X] min
- **CQRS pairing:** Event sourcing paired with CQRS — command side writes events; query side reads projections

## 9. Messaging Architecture

### 9.1 Messaging Topology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Kafka["Apache Kafka Cluster"]
        subgraph Topics["Topics (partitioned, replicated)"]
            T1[orders.v1\n[X] partitions]
            T2[payments.v1\n[X] partitions]
            T3[notifications.v1\n[X] partitions]
            T4[audit.v1\n[X] partitions]
        end
        DLQ[Dead Letter Topics\n*.dlq]
        SR[Schema Registry\nAvro / Protobuf schemas]
    end

    subgraph Queues["Point-to-Point Queues (SQS / RabbitMQ)"]
        Q1[email-send-queue]
        Q2[sms-send-queue]
        Q3[pdf-generation-queue]
    end

    PROD[Producers] --> Topics
    Topics --> CONS[Consumers\nConsumer Groups]
    Topics -.-> DLQ
    PROD --> Queues
    Queues --> WORKERS[Workers]
```

### 9.2 Kafka Configuration Standards

| Parameter | Value | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Replication factor | [3] | Tolerate [1] broker failure |
| Min in-sync replicas | [2] | No data loss on producer ack |
| Retention (default) | [7 days] | Allow consumer replay window |
| Retention (audit) | [365 days] | Compliance requirement |
| Partitions (default) | [X] per topic | Based on peak throughput target |
| Compression | Snappy | Balance CPU and network |
| Consumer offset | Committed after processing | At-least-once semantics |
| Exactly-once semantics | Enabled for [financial events] | Where duplicates are harmful |
| Max message size | [X] MB | Reject oversized payloads |

### 9.3 Message Delivery Guarantees

| Guarantee | Description | When to Use | How Achieved |
|---|---|---|---|
| At-most-once | May lose messages; no duplicates | Telemetry, non-critical metrics | Fire-and-forget, no retries |
| At-least-once | No message loss; may duplicate | Most business events (default) | Producer ack + consumer retry |
| Exactly-once | No loss, no duplicates | Financial, inventory, billing | Kafka transactions + idempotent consumer |

### 9.4 Dead Letter Queue (DLQ) Strategy

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    MSG[Message] --> CONS[Consumer]
    CONS -- success --> COMMIT[Commit Offset]
    CONS -- fail --> RETRY{Retry\n< max attempts?}
    RETRY -- yes --> CONS
    RETRY -- no --> DLQ[Dead Letter Topic\n*.dlq]
    DLQ --> ALERT[Alert: Slack / PagerDuty]
    DLQ --> INSPECT[Human Inspection\nor automated reprocessor]
    INSPECT --> REPLAY[Replay to\noriginal topic]
```

**DLQ standards:**
- Every consumer topic has a corresponding `*.dlq` topic
- DLQ message retains original message + metadata: `failure_reason`, `attempt_count`, `first_failed_at`
- Alert fires when DLQ depth > [X] messages for [X] minutes
- DLQ messages reviewed within [X] hours (P1) or [X] business days (P2/P3)
- DLQ retention: [X] days

### 10.1 File Integration Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart LR

    end

    subgraph Outbound["Outbound File Generation"]
        GEN[Generate File\nFrom DB / API]
        ENCRYPT[Encrypt / Sign\nPGP / GPG]

        ACK[Acknowledgement\nFunctional ACK / 997]
    end

    Inbound --> DETECT
    DETECT --> VALID
    VALID --> PARSE
    PARSE --> LOAD
    LOAD --> GEN
    GEN --> ENCRYPT
    ENCRYPT --> DELIVER
    DELIVER --> ACK
```

### 10.2 Supported File Formats

| Format | Use Case | Schema Validation | Tooling |
|---|---|---|---|
| CSV | Bulk data exchange, reports | Column header + type check | [Pandas / Spark / Great Expectations] |
| JSON | API payloads, event snapshots | JSON Schema | [AJV / jsonschema] |
| XML | Legacy and EDI-adjacent systems | XSD | [JAXB / lxml] |
| Parquet | Bulk analytics data transfer | Schema on read | [Spark / dbt] |
| EDI X12 | B2B supply chain (orders, invoices) | X12 spec validation | [iPaaS / Stedi] |
| EDIFACT | EU B2B partner exchange | EDIFACT spec | [iPaaS / custom] |
| PDF | Human-readable reports and invoices | N/A | [Generated, not parsed] |
| Fixed-Width | Legacy mainframe systems | Position-based spec | [Custom parser] |

### 10.3 File Transfer Standards

- **Protocol:** SFTP (preferred), FTPS (legacy only); FTP never permitted
- **Encryption:** PGP/GPG encryption for files containing PII or confidential data
- **Integrity:** MD5 / SHA-256 checksum file delivered alongside data file; validated before processing
- **Naming convention:** `[source]_[entity]_[YYYYMMDD]_[HHMMSS]_[seq].csv`
- **Acknowledgement:** Functional ACK returned within [X] minutes of successful processing
- **Error notification:** Failed file processing alerts partner within [X] hours with error detail

### 11.2 When to Use iPaaS vs Custom Integration

| Scenario | Recommendation | Rationale |
|---|---|---|
| Connecting two SaaS products (e.g., Salesforce ↔ HubSpot) | iPaaS | Pre-built connectors; no custom code |
| Legacy EDI or mainframe integration | iPaaS | Complex protocol support built-in |
| High-throughput, low-latency internal service calls | Custom (service mesh + direct API) | iPaaS adds latency overhead |
| Complex transformation between many formats | iPaaS | Visual mapping tools; maintainable by non-developers |
| New microservice integration (greenfield) | Custom REST/gRPC + event | Full control, no iPaaS dependency |
| Simple SaaS webhook → internal event bridge | iPaaS or lightweight Lambda | Low effort, no infra to manage |

## 14. Identity & Access in Integrations

### 14.1 Authentication Flows

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLIENT as Client / Service
    participant IDP as Identity Provider (Auth0 / Okta)
    participant GW as API Gateway
    participant SVC as Backend Service

    Note over CLIENT,SVC: Machine-to-Machine (M2M) — Client Credentials Flow
    CLIENT->>IDP: POST /oauth/token\n{client_id, client_secret, grant_type}
    IDP-->>CLIENT: access_token (JWT, short-lived)
    CLIENT->>GW: API Request\nAuthorization: Bearer {access_token}
    GW->>IDP: Validate token (JWKS / introspection)
    IDP-->>GW: Token valid + claims
    GW->>SVC: Forward request + claims header
    SVC-->>CLIENT: Response

    Note over CLIENT,SVC: User-Delegated — Authorization Code + PKCE Flow
    CLIENT->>IDP: Redirect to /authorize
    IDP-->>CLIENT: Auth code (after user login + consent)
    CLIENT->>IDP: POST /oauth/token\n{code, code_verifier}
    IDP-->>CLIENT: access_token + refresh_token
    CLIENT->>GW: API Request with access_token
    GW->>SVC: Forward with user claims
```

### 14.2 Authentication Methods by Integration Type

| Integration Type | Auth Method | Token Type | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| External user-facing API | OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code + PKCE | JWT (15 min) | Via IdP (Auth0 / Okta) |
| Machine-to-machine (internal) | OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials | JWT (1h) | Service accounts per service |
| Partner API | OAuth 2.0 Client Credentials or API Key | JWT or opaque | Scoped to partner permissions |
| Third-party SaaS (outbound) | API Key / OAuth | Varies | Keys in Secrets Manager |
| Webhook (inbound) | HMAC signature verification | `X-Signature` header | Secret per webhook source |
| B2B / EDI partner | mTLS client certificate | X.509 cert | Cert rotation on expiry |
| Internal service mesh | mTLS (automatic via mesh) | SPIFFE/SVID | Zero manual token management |

### 14.3 API Key Management

- **Storage:** Keys stored in [Vault / AWS Secrets Manager]; never in source code or config files
- **Rotation:** Automated rotation every [X] days; overlap period [X] days
- **Scoping:** Keys scoped to minimum required permissions; no all-access keys
- **Audit:** All key usage logged; unused keys flagged after [X] days
- **Revocation:** Keys revocable within [X] minutes; consumers notified via [channel]

## 15. Data Transformation & Mapping

### 15.1 Transformation Patterns

| Pattern | Description | Tool / Approach | Use Case |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Field mapping** | Source field → target field (rename, reorder) | JSONata / DataWeave / iPaaS mapper | Most common; SaaS sync |
| **Type coercion** | Convert data types (string → int, date formats) | Transform scripts | Cross-system date/type normalization |
| **Aggregation** | Combine multiple source records into one | Custom / dbt | Rollups, summaries |
| **Splitting** | One source record → multiple target records | Custom / iPaaS | Line item expansion |
| **Enrichment** | Add data from a lookup source | Service call / reference table | Geo lookup, customer tier |
| **Filter** | Exclude records that don't meet criteria | iPaaS filter / code | Conditional routing |
| **Format conversion** | JSON ↔ XML ↔ CSV ↔ EDI ↔ Protobuf | XSLT / DataWeave / custom | Cross-protocol exchange |
| **Canonical model** | Translate to/from a neutral internal model | Custom canonical schema | Decouple source from target |

### 15.2 Canonical Data Model

> A canonical (neutral) data model insulates internal services from external schema changes.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph Sources["External Formats"]
        SF[Salesforce Contact JSON]
        SAP[SAP Customer IDOC]
        CSV[Partner CSV]
    end

    subgraph Canonical["Canonical Customer Model\n(Internal schema)"]
        CDM[customer_id\nfull_name\nemail\nphone\naddress\nsource_system\nsource_id\ncreated_at\nupdated_at]
    end

    subgraph Targets["Internal Consumers"]
        CRM[Internal CRM Service]
        DWH[Data Warehouse]
        NOTIFY[Notification Service]
    end

    SF -->|Inbound adapter| Canonical
    SAP -->|Inbound adapter| Canonical
    CSV -->|Inbound adapter| Canonical

    Canonical -->|Outbound adapter| CRM
    Canonical -->|Outbound adapter| DWH
    Canonical -->|Outbound adapter| NOTIFY
```

### 15.3 Schema & Contract Management

- **API contracts:** OpenAPI 3.x stored in Git; versioned alongside service code
- **Event schemas:** Avro / Protobuf registered in Schema Registry; compatibility mode: `BACKWARD`
- **Schema evolution rules:**
  - Adding optional fields → BACKWARD compatible — allowed
  - Removing fields → FORWARD incompatible — requires deprecation period + new version
  - Renaming fields → BREAKING — new version required
- **Contract testing:** [Pact] consumer-driven contract tests run in CI; producer cannot deploy if consumer contracts break

## 16. Error Handling & Resilience

### 16.1 Resilience Patterns

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    CALL[Outbound Integration Call]

    CALL --> TIMEOUT{Timeout\nexceeded?}
    TIMEOUT -- yes --> FALLBACK[Fallback Response\nor Graceful Degradation]
    TIMEOUT -- no --> CB{Circuit Breaker\nOPEN?}

    CB -- open --> FALLBACK
    CB -- closed/half-open --> RETRY{Retry\npossible?}

    RETRY -- yes --> BACKOFF[Exponential Backoff\n+ Jitter]
    BACKOFF --> CALL
    RETRY -- no\nor max retries --> DLQ_OR_ERROR[DLQ or Error Response]

    DLQ_OR_ERROR --> ALERT[Alert / Log]
    FALLBACK --> LOG[Log degradation event]
```

### 16.2 Resilience Configuration Standards

| Pattern | Default Config | Override? | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|
| Timeout | [X]ms for sync REST; [X]ms for gRPC | Per-route | Never unlimited |
| Retry | Max [3] attempts; exponential backoff [100ms, 200ms, 400ms] + jitter | Per-route | Not on POST (unless idempotent) |
| Circuit Breaker | Trip: [50]% errors in [10]s window; half-open: [30]s; close: [2] successes | Per upstream | All external calls |
| Bulkhead | Separate thread/connection pool per downstream service | Per service | Prevent noisy-neighbour saturation |
| Rate Limit (inbound) | [X] req/s per consumer at gateway | Per consumer tier | Protect internal services |
| Rate Limit (outbound) | Respect upstream rate limits; back-pressure via queue | Per external API | Especially third-party SaaS |
| Idempotency | All POST/PATCH endpoints accept `Idempotency-Key` header | Required for financial ops | Key TTL: [X] hours |
| Fallback | Return cached, degraded, or empty response | Per use case | Documented per API |

### 16.3 Idempotency Implementation

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CLIENT as Client
    participant API as API Service
    participant STORE as Idempotency Store (Redis)
    participant DB as Database

    CLIENT->>API: POST /orders\nIdempotency-Key: {uuid}
    API->>STORE: Get key {uuid}
    alt Key exists (duplicate request)
        STORE-->>API: Cached response
        API-->>CLIENT: 200 + original response
    else Key not found (first attempt)
        STORE-->>API: null
        API->>DB: Create order
        DB-->>API: Order created
        API->>STORE: Store key → response (TTL: 24h)
        API-->>CLIENT: 201 Created
    end
```

## 17. Integration Security

### 17.1 Security Controls at Integration Boundaries

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    EXT[External Traffic] --> WAF[WAF\nOWASP rule set, DDoS protection]
    WAF --> TLS[TLS Termination\nTLS 1.2+ only]
    TLS --> GW[API Gateway\nAuthn, rate limiting, IP filtering]
    GW --> AUTHZ[Authorization\nJWT claims, RBAC, scopes]
    AUTHZ --> VALID[Input Validation\nSchema, size, injection checks]
    VALID --> SVC[Backend Service]

    SVC --> OUT[Outbound Calls]
    OUT --> EGRESS[Egress Control\nAllowlist of approved destinations]
    EGRESS --> DEST[Upstream / Partner]
```

### 17.2 OWASP API Security Top 10 Mitigations

| # | Threat | Mitigation |
|---|---|---|
| API1 | Broken Object Level Authorization | Per-object ownership check in service layer; not just role |
| API2 | Broken Authentication | Short-lived JWTs; MFA for sensitive operations; no API keys in URLs |
| API3 | Broken Object Property Level Authorization | Response filtering; never expose internal fields |
| API4 | Unrestricted Resource Consumption | Rate limiting + quota at gateway; request size limits |
| API5 | Broken Function Level Authorization | Explicit authorization per HTTP method; deny by default |
| API6 | Unrestricted Access to Sensitive Flows | Step-up auth for sensitive flows; CAPTCHA where appropriate |
| API7 | Server-Side Request Forgery (SSRF) | Allowlist outbound domains; no user-controlled redirect targets |
| API8 | Security Misconfiguration | IaC-managed gateway config; no debug endpoints in prod |
| API9 | Improper Inventory Management | Full API catalog; deprecated APIs removed; no shadow APIs |
| API10 | Unsafe Consumption of APIs | Validate and sanitize all third-party API responses |

### 17.3 Sensitive Data in Integrations

- **PII in transit:** TLS 1.2+ required; PII fields masked in logs (email → `[REDACTED]`)
- **PII in events:** Restricted Kafka topics; event consumers must be authorized
- **Payment data:** Never stored or logged; tokenized via payment gateway before reaching platform
- **Secrets in integration configs:** All credentials in [Vault / Secrets Manager]; never in code or iPaaS flow configs
- **Webhook signatures:** All inbound webhooks validated via HMAC-SHA256 before processing

### 18.2 SaaS Integration Design

**Inbound webhooks from SaaS:**
1. Register webhook endpoint on SaaS platform
2. Validate HMAC signature on every inbound request
3. Return `200 OK` immediately; process asynchronously via queue
4. Implement idempotency using event ID from SaaS payload
5. Store raw webhook payload before processing (for replay)

**Outbound calls to SaaS:**
1. All credentials in Secrets Manager; rotated per policy
2. Calls via circuit breaker; fallback if SaaS is unavailable
3. Retry with exponential backoff; respect `Retry-After` headers
4. Structured log all calls (status, latency, SaaS request ID)

## 20. Legacy & On-Premises Integration

### 20.1 Legacy Integration Patterns

| Pattern | Description | When to Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Strangler Fig** | Route traffic progressively to new service; shrink legacy | Incremental monolith replacement |
| **Anti-Corruption Layer (ACL)** | Adapter translates legacy model to internal canonical model | Isolate domain from legacy schema pollution |
| **Database Integration** | Read/write directly to legacy DB (read-only preferred) | Legacy has no API; short-term only |
| **File Exchange** | Legacy drops/picks up files from shared location | Batch-oriented legacy systems |
| **Message Bridge** | Legacy MQ (IBM MQ, MSMQ) bridged to modern broker (Kafka) | Mainframe / mid-range systems |
| **ETL / CDC** | Extract data from legacy DB into modern platform | Data migration, shadow running |

## 21. Integration Testing Strategy

### 21.1 Testing Layers

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Tests["Integration Testing Pyramid"]
        E2E[End-to-End Tests\nFull flow across systems\nSmall number, high value]
        CONTRACT[Contract Tests\nConsumer-driven — Pact\nPer consumer/producer pair]
        INTEGRATION[Integration Tests\nService + real or stubbed dependencies\nFocus: happy path + key error paths]
        UNIT[Unit Tests\nTransformation logic\nMessage parsing / mapping]
    end

    UNIT --> INTEGRATION --> CONTRACT --> E2E
```

### 21.2 Contract Testing (Consumer-Driven)

```mermaid
sequenceDiagram
    participant CONS as Consumer Team
    participant PACT as Pact Broker
    participant PROD as Producer Team
    participant CI as CI/CD Pipeline

    CONS->>PACT: Publish consumer contract\n(expected request/response shape)
    PACT-->>PROD: Notify: new contract version
    PROD->>PACT: Fetch consumer contracts
    PROD->>CI: Run provider verification\nagainst consumer contracts
    CI-->>PACT: Publish verification results
    PACT-->>CI: can-i-deploy check\n(pass if contracts verified)
    CI-->>PROD: Deploy approved
```

### 21.3 Test Types & Tooling

| Test Type | Purpose | Tool | Run In | Gate |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Unit tests | Transform logic, mapping, parsing | [Jest / pytest / JUnit] | CI (every PR) | Block merge |
| Integration tests | Service + real dependencies (dev env) | [Postman / RestAssured / Supertest] | CI (every PR) | Block merge |
| Contract tests | Consumer / producer API contracts | [Pact] | CI (every PR) | Block deploy |
| Component tests | Service in isolation with mocked deps | [WireMock / Mockoon] | CI (every PR) | Block merge |
| End-to-end tests | Full flow across systems | [Playwright / Cypress / custom] | Staging (on deploy) | Block prod |
| Load / performance tests | Integration under load | [k6 / Gatling] | Staging (weekly) | Advisory |
| Chaos tests | Failure injection | [Chaos Monkey / Gremlin] | Staging (scheduled) | Advisory |
| Security tests (DAST) | API security scanning | [OWASP ZAP / Burp Suite] | Staging (weekly) | Block prod |

### 21.4 Test Environment Strategy

| Environment | Purpose | Dependencies | Data |
|---|---|---|---|
| `local` | Developer unit + component tests | Mocked / WireMock stubs | Synthetic |
| `dev` | Integration tests; team PR validation | Real services (dev instances) + some stubs | Synthetic |
| `staging` | End-to-end, load, security tests | Full integration (sandbox / non-prod SaaS) | Anonymized prod-like |
| `production` | Synthetic monitoring / canary | Real production systems | Production |

### 22.2 Distributed Tracing Standards

- **Standard:** OpenTelemetry (OTEL) — vendor-neutral
- **Propagation format:** W3C TraceContext (`traceparent` header)
- **Required fields:** `trace_id`, `span_id`, `parent_span_id`, `service_name`, `operation_name`, `status_code`, `duration_ms`
- **Sampling:** 100% for error traces; [X]% for high-volume success traces
- **Cross-system correlation:** `trace_id` propagated into async messages as CloudEvents extension; into batch files as metadata field
- **Storage:** [Jaeger / Tempo / Datadog APM] — [X] day retention

### 22.3 Key Integration Metrics

| Metric | Description | Target | Alert Threshold |
|---|---|---|---|
| API success rate | % 2xx responses | > 99.9% | < 99.5% over 5 min |
| API p99 latency | 99th percentile response time | < [X]ms | > [X]ms over 5 min |
| Event processing lag | Kafka consumer lag (messages behind) | < [X]K messages | > [X]K for [X] min |
| Event processing error rate | % messages sent to DLQ | < 0.1% | > 0.5% over 5 min |
| iPaaS flow success rate | % flows completing without error | > 99% | < 98% over 15 min |
| File processing SLA | % files processed within SLA | > 99% | Any breach |
| Circuit breaker state | Number of open circuit breakers | 0 | > 0 for [X] min |
| DLQ depth | Messages in dead letter queues | 0 | > [X] messages |

## 24. Governance & Lifecycle Management

### 24.1 Integration Governance Framework

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph Intake["Intake & Design"]
        REQ_G[Integration Request]
        REVIEW_G[Architecture Review\nPattern selection, security, contract]
        APPROVE_G[Approval]
    end

    subgraph Build["Build & Test"]
        CONTRACT_G[Contract defined\nOpenAPI / AsyncAPI]
        IMPLEMENT_G[Implementation]
        TEST_G[Integration tests\nContract tests]
    end

    subgraph Operate["Operate & Maintain"]
        MONITOR_G[Production monitoring\nSLA tracking]
        CHANGE_G[Change management\nBreaking change process]
        DEPRECATE_G[Deprecation process\nSunset headers, comms, migration]
    end

    Intake --> Build --> Operate
    Operate -->|New major change| Intake
```

### 24.2 API Lifecycle Stages

| Stage | Description | Key Actions |
|---|---|---|
| **Design** | Define contract; review with consumers | OpenAPI spec drafted; design review complete |
| **Development** | Implement against the contract | Contract tests written; mock server available |
| **Beta** | Early access for select consumers | Flagged as beta; breaking changes allowed with notice |
| **GA (Stable)** | Production-ready; SLA-backed | No breaking changes; versioning enforced |
| **Deprecated** | Superseded by new version | `Deprecation` + `Sunset` headers; migration guide published |
| **Retired** | Removed from service | Traffic = 0; endpoint removed; consumers migrated |

### 24.3 Breaking Change Process

1. **Identify** — determine if change is breaking (see versioning rules in Section 7.3)
2. **New version** — create `v[N+1]` alongside existing version
3. **Communicate** — notify all known consumers [X] months in advance via changelog + email + portal
4. **Migration guide** — publish documentation for consumer migration steps
5. **Monitoring** — track consumer migration progress via API gateway analytics
6. **Sunset** — set `Sunset` header on deprecated version; remove on [date]
7. **Retire** — decommission old version after [X]-month support window

### 24.4 Integration Ownership Model

| Asset | Owner Responsibility | Escalation Path |
|---|---|---|
| API / endpoint | Producing service team | Integration architect → CTO |
| Event topic | Publishing service team | Integration architect → CTO |
| iPaaS flow | Integration / platform team | Integration architect |
| Partner integration | Partner engineering team | Partner lead → CTO |
| API gateway config | Platform team | Platform lead |
